prompt:
The repository contains brackets.py with a broken `is_balanced` function. It is meant to return True when every opening bracket in the string has a correctly ordered, correctly typed matching closer, and False otherwise. Two bugs exist: (a) it never checks that a closer matches the most recent opener, so wrongly-interleaved input like '([)]' is accepted; (b) it calls stack.pop() on a closer even when the stack is empty, which raises IndexError on input such as ']' or ')('. Fix the function so ALL of these hold: it returns True for correctly nested brackets (and for strings with non-bracket characters mixed in), returns False for mismatched, interleaved, or unbalanced input, and NEVER raises on an unexpected closer. Non-bracket characters are ignored. Return the COMPLETE corrected contents of brackets.py using EXACTLY this format (the delimiters must be on their own lines):
<<<FILE brackets.py>>>
<full file content here>
<<<END>>>
Return nothing else.

inputs.name: brackets.py (current, buggy)

testFiles.content:
from brackets import is_balanced

assert is_balanced('([])') is True
assert is_balanced('([]{})') is True
assert is_balanced('') is True
assert is_balanced('abc') is True
assert is_balanced('(a[b]c)') is True
assert is_balanced('([)]') is False, 'interleaved must fail'
assert is_balanced('(') is False
assert is_balanced(']') is False, 'lone closer must not crash'
assert is_balanced(')(') is False
assert is_balanced('(()') is False
print('ok')
